questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual attribute inheritance in C++. Why is it important for achieving runtime polymorphism?
keyPoints: Virtual attribute is automatically inherited by overriding functions
Enables runtime polymorphism through dynamic binding
Function call resolved based on actual object type not pointer type
All derived classes maintaining virtual nature without explicit declaration

questions.question: What is the difference between function overriding and function hiding? Give examples.
keyPoints: Overriding: replaces base virtual function implementation
Hiding: occurs when derived function has different signature
Hiding hides ALL base versions regardless of signature
Override requires virtual function in base class

questions.question: Explain the purpose of vtable and vptr in implementing virtual functions.
keyPoints: vtable: lookup table with function pointers to virtual functions
vptr: hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable
Compiler generates these for classes with virtual functions
Enables runtime polymorphism through indirect function call

questions.question: Why are virtual destructors necessary in polymorphic base classes?
keyPoints: Ensures proper cleanup of derived class resources
Without virtual, only base destructor gets called when deleting through base pointer
Causes memory leaks in derived class allocations
Prevents undefined behavior during object destruction

questions.question: What is an abstract class? Can we create objects of an abstract class? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function
Pure virtual declared with '= 0' syntax
Cannot instantiate abstract class - compiler error
Purpose is to define interface for derived classes

questions.question: Explain the concept of slicing in C++. How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Slicing occurs when derived object assigned to base by value
Derived portion is 'sliced off' - loses polymorphic behavior
To prevent: use pointers or references to base class
Use virtual functions only through pointers/references

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'override' and 'final' keywords in C++11?
keyPoints: override: explicitly indicates function overrides base virtual function
Compiler catches errors if no matching base function exists
final: prevents further overriding of virtual function
Both help write correct polymorphic code at compile-time

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for a shape drawing system using virtual functions. Include a base class Shape with virtual function draw(), and derived classes Circle and Rectangle. Demonstrate runtime polymorphism using base class pointers.
keyPoints: Define Shape as base class with virtual draw() function
Create Circle and Rectangle derived classes overriding draw()
Implement base class pointer pointing to derived objects
Call draw() through base pointer showing polymorphic behavior
Include virtual destructor in base class
Proper code structure and demonstration of polymorphism

questions.question: Explain the complete process of how a virtual function call works at runtime in C++. Include vtable, vptr, and dynamic dispatch mechanism in your explanation.
keyPoints: Compiler creates vtable for each class with virtual functions
Each object contains hidden vptr pointing to class vtable
Virtual function call goes through vptr to vtable to actual function
Resolution happens at runtime based on actual object type
Diagram explanation of lookup process
Memory overhead explanation

questions.question: Create an abstract class Animal with pure virtual function speak(). Derive classes Dog and Cat that implement the speak() function. Write a main function demonstrating polymorphism using Animal pointers.
keyPoints: Define Animal as abstract class with pure virtual speak()
Implement Dog class returning appropriate sound
Implement Cat class returning appropriate sound
Create array of Animal pointers pointing to different derived objects
Demonstrate polymorphic calls through base pointers
Proper virtual destructor in base class
Code should compile and demonstrate runtime polymorphism

questions.question: Explain how constructor and destructor calls work in an inheritance hierarchy with virtual functions. Why is the order important? What happens if base class destructor is not virtual?
keyPoints: Constructors called from base to derived (bottom-up)
Destructors called from derived to base (top-down)
Ensures proper initialization and cleanup order
Non-virtual destructor: only base destructor called on delete through base pointer
Derived class resources not freed causing memory leaks
Demonstrate with code example showing proper cleanup
